news_summary: Recent advances in technology showcase a major breakthrough in MXene materials dramatically enhancing conductivity and the development of a quantum battery that revolutionizes charging efficiency.
news_summary_fr: Les récentes avancées technologiques ont permis une percée majeure dans le domaine des matériaux MXène, qui améliorent considérablement la conductivité, ainsi que le développement d'une batterie quantique qui révolutionne l'efficacité de la recharge.
news_summary_es: Los recientes avances tecnológicos ponen de manifiesto un gran avance en los materiales MXene, que mejoran notablemente la conductividad, así como el desarrollo de una batería cuántica que revoluciona la eficiencia de la recarga.

articles.title: MXene breakthrough boosts conductivity 160x with perfect atomic order
articles.summary: A new breakthrough is transforming MXenes—ultra-thin, high-tech materials—into something far more powerful and precise. Researchers have developed a cleaner, more controlled way to build these materials using molten salts and iodine, eliminating the messy chemical processes that once left their surfaces disordered. The result is a perfectly arranged atomic structure that lets electrons flow with remarkable ease, boosting conductivity by up to 160 times.
articles.content:
Discovered in 2011, MXenes are a fast-expanding family of ultra-thin inorganic materials. They are made from stacked layers of transition metals combined with carbon or nitrogen, with atoms attached to their outer surfaces. These surface atoms are not just decorative. They play a central role in how the material behaves. "They strongly influence how electrons move through the material, how stable it is, and how it interacts with light, heat, and chemical environments," explains Dr. Mahdi Ghorbani-Asl from the Institute of Ion Beam Physics and Materials Research at HZDR.

Until now, most MXenes have been produced using chemical etching, a process that leaves a mix of surface atoms such as oxygen, fluorine, or chlorine scattered randomly across the material. This lack of order creates problems. "This atomic disorder limits performance because it traps and scatters electrons, much like potholes slowing traffic on a highway," describes Dr. Dongqi Li from TU Dresden.

Cleaner Synthesis With Precise Surface Control

A new technique known as the GLS method takes a very different approach. Instead of relying on harsh chemicals, it starts with solid materials called MAX phases and uses molten salts along with iodine vapor to form MXene sheets. This process allows researchers to control which halogen atoms, including chlorine, bromine, or iodine, attach to the surface.

The result is a much cleaner material. The surface atoms are arranged in a uniform and highly ordered way, and unwanted impurities are greatly reduced. The team demonstrated the versatility of this approach by successfully producing MXenes from eight different MAX phases.

To better understand how these surface changes affect performance, the researchers also used density functional theory (DFT) calculations. These simulations provided detailed insight into how different surface terminations influence both stability and electronic behavior. "By combining theory with our experimental ability to precisely control surface terminations, we open a new path toward MXenes with improved stability and tailored functional properties," concludes Ghorbani-Asl.

Dramatic Gains in Conductivity and Electron Mobility

To highlight the impact of the new method, the team focused on titanium carbide MXene Ti 3 C 2 , one of the most widely studied examples. When produced using conventional techniques, this material typically contains a mix of chlorine and oxygen on its surface, which interferes with its electrical performance. With the GLS method, however, the researchers created Ti 3 C 2 Cl 2 , a version with only chlorine atoms arranged in a clean, ordered structure and no detectable impurities.

"The results were striking. The chlorine-terminated MXene variant showed a 160-fold increase in macroscopic conductivity and a 13-fold enhancement in terahertz conductivity compared with the same material made by traditional methods. In addition, a nearly fourfold increase in charge carrier mobility was observed, a key measure of how freely electrons move through a material," Li summarizes.

These improvements come directly from the smoother, more consistent surface. With fewer disruptions, electrons can travel more freely across the material. Quantum transport simulations confirmed that the ordered structure reduces electron trapping and scattering, offering a clear explanation for the observed performance boost.

Customizing MXenes for Future Technologies

The benefits go beyond electrical conductivity. The study also shows that changing the type of halogen on the surface alters how MXenes interact with electromagnetic waves. This makes it possible to design materials for specific uses, including radar-absorbing coatings, electromagnetic shielding, and advanced wireless technologies. For instance, chlorine-terminated MXenes absorb strongly in the 14-18 GHz range, while bromine- and iodine-based versions respond to different frequency ranges.

The GLS method also opens the door to even more customization. By combining different halide salts, researchers created MXenes with two or even three types of surface halogens in carefully controlled proportions. This ability to fine-tune surface composition provides a powerful new way to design materials for electronics, catalysis, energy storage, photonics, and other applications.

A Major Step Forward for MXene Chemistry

Overall, this work marks an important advance in the field of MXenes. It introduces a gentler and widely applicable way to produce materials with highly ordered surfaces and precisely controlled chemistry. According to the researchers, this approach could speed up the development of next-generation technologies, including flexible electronics, high-speed communication systems, and advanced optoelectronic devices.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough in MXene material synthesis that dramatically improves conductivity and electron mobility by up to 160 times through a cleaner, more controlled method. This advancement has broad implications for future technologies such as flexible electronics, wireless communication, and energy storage, representing a meaningful and tangible benefit to society. The article is focused, detailed, and highlights both the method and its wide-ranging potential applications.
articles.personality_title: New method boosts MXene conductivity by 160 times through perfect atomic order
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – MXenes are very thin materials made of metals and carbon or nitrogen. Since their discovery in 2011, they have been used in many high-tech fields. However, the usual way to make MXenes uses chemicals that leave their surfaces messy and disorganized. This disorder slows down how well electricity moves through them.

**What happened** – Scientists developed a cleaner method called the GLS method. Instead of harsh chemicals, they use molten salts and iodine vapor to carefully control which atoms attach to the MXene surface. This creates a perfectly ordered atomic structure, free from impurities. They tested this on a common MXene called titanium carbide (Ti3C2) and made a version with only chlorine atoms on the surface.

**Impact** – This cleaner, ordered surface lets electrons flow much more easily. The new MXene showed 160 times higher electrical conductivity and four times better electron mobility than traditional versions. This means electricity moves through it much faster and with less resistance. The method also allows scientists to change the surface atoms to suit different uses, such as absorbing radar waves or improving wireless signals.

**What's next step** – Researchers plan to use this method to create MXenes with different combinations of surface atoms. This will help design materials for flexible electronics, faster communication devices, energy storage, and more. The technique could speed up making better MXenes for many future technologies.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A new clean method for making MXenes creates perfectly ordered surfaces that improve electrical conductivity by 160 times, enabling better materials for future technology.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une nouvelle méthode augmente la conductivité des MXènes de 160 fois grâce à un ordre atomique parfait
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les MXènes sont des matériaux très fins composés de métaux et de carbone ou d'azote. Depuis leur découverte en 2011, ils sont utilisés dans de nombreux domaines technologiques. Cependant, la méthode habituelle de fabrication utilise des produits chimiques qui laissent leurs surfaces désordonnées. Ce désordre ralentit le passage de l'électricité.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des scientifiques ont développé une méthode plus propre appelée méthode GLS. Au lieu de produits chimiques agressifs, ils utilisent des sels fondus et de la vapeur d'iode pour contrôler précisément quels atomes se fixent à la surface des MXènes. Cela crée une structure atomique parfaitement ordonnée, sans impuretés. Ils ont testé cette méthode sur un MXène courant, le carbure de titane (Ti3C2), en obtenant une version avec seulement des atomes de chlore à la surface.

**Impact** – Cette surface propre et ordonnée permet aux électrons de circuler beaucoup plus facilement. Le nouveau MXène présente une conductivité électrique 160 fois plus élevée et une mobilité des électrons quatre fois meilleure que les versions traditionnelles. Cela signifie que l'électricité circule plus vite et avec moins de résistance. La méthode permet aussi de changer les atomes de surface pour s'adapter à différents usages, comme absorber les ondes radar ou améliorer les signaux sans fil.

**Étape suivante** – Les chercheurs vont utiliser cette méthode pour créer des MXènes avec différentes combinaisons d'atomes de surface. Cela aidera à concevoir des matériaux pour l'électronique flexible, des appareils de communication plus rapides, le stockage d'énergie, et plus encore. Cette technique pourrait accélérer la fabrication de meilleurs MXènes pour de nombreuses technologies futures.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Une nouvelle méthode propre de fabrication des MXènes crée des surfaces parfaitement ordonnées qui améliorent la conductivité électrique de 160 fois, ouvrant la voie à de meilleurs matériaux pour les technologies futures.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevo método aumenta la conductividad de MXenes 160 veces con orden atómico perfecto
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los MXenes son materiales muy delgados hechos de metales y carbono o nitrógeno. Desde su descubrimiento en 2011, se usan en muchas áreas tecnológicas. Sin embargo, la forma habitual de fabricarlos usa productos químicos que dejan sus superficies desordenadas, lo que reduce el paso de la electricidad.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos desarrollaron un método más limpio llamado método GLS. En lugar de químicos fuertes, usan sales fundidas y vapor de yodo para controlar qué átomos se unen a la superficie del MXene. Esto crea una estructura atómica perfectamente ordenada, sin impurezas. Lo probaron en un MXene común llamado carburo de titanio (Ti3C2), obteniendo una versión con solo átomos de cloro en la superficie.

**Impacto** – Esta superficie limpia y ordenada permite que los electrones se muevan mucho más fácilmente. El nuevo MXene mostró una conductividad eléctrica 160 veces mayor y una movilidad de electrones cuatro veces mejor que las versiones tradicionales. Esto significa que la electricidad pasa más rápido y con menos resistencia. El método también permite cambiar los átomos de la superficie para diferentes usos, como absorber ondas de radar o mejorar señales inalámbricas.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores planean usar este método para crear MXenes con diferentes combinaciones de átomos en la superficie. Esto ayudará a diseñar materiales para electrónica flexible, dispositivos de comunicación más rápidos, almacenamiento de energía y más. La técnica podría acelerar la producción de mejores MXenes para muchas tecnologías futuras.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Un nuevo método limpio para fabricar MXenes crea superficies perfectamente ordenadas que mejoran la conductividad eléctrica 160 veces, facilitando mejores materiales para tecnología futura.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of ultra-thin layered sheets with perfectly aligned, glowing atomic spheres on their surfaces, symbolizing ordered halogen atoms; smooth, bright electron waves flow effortlessly across the layers like clear, flowing streams, all set against a softly glowing abstract background evoking advanced materials and cutting-edge technology.

articles.title: Scientists built a quantum battery that breaks the rules of charging
articles.summary: Scientists have taken a major step toward futuristic energy tech by building a working prototype of a quantum battery—one that can charge, store, and release energy using the strange rules of quantum physics instead of chemistry. This tiny, laser-powered device hints at a future where energy storage is not only faster but actually improves as systems get larger, flipping the rules of conventional batteries.
articles.content:
Scientists have taken an important step toward next-generation energy technology by developing a proof-of-concept quantum battery that can charge, store, and release energy. This early prototype represents the closest progress so far toward building a fully functional quantum battery.

Unlike conventional batteries that depend on chemical reactions, quantum batteries rely on the unusual principles of quantum physics. They use effects such as superposition and interactions between light and electrons, which could allow for much faster charging and greater energy storage capacity.

Although practical quantum batteries are not yet available, advances like this suggest they could eventually reshape how energy is stored and delivered.

The research was led by CSIRO -- Australia's national science agency -- in collaboration with RMIT University and the University of Melbourne. The findings were published on March 13 in Light: Science & Applications.

Quantum Batteries Get Faster as They Grow

Study co-author and RMIT PhD candidate Daniel Tibben said the team uncovered a surprising advantage.

"Our study found quantum batteries charge faster as they get larger, which is not how today's batteries work," Tibben said.

"It's a sign that quantum batteries could one day outperform conventional energy-storage technologies."

This behavior contrasts sharply with traditional batteries, which do not gain efficiency as they increase in size. The discovery highlights a potential path toward more powerful and efficient energy systems.

A Working Prototype Demonstrates Key Functions

Co-author Daniel Gómez, an RMIT Professor of Chemical Physics, explained that the device marks a major milestone.

"We demonstrated a device that can be charged, store that energy and then discharge it," Gómez said.

"This is an exciting development in a rapidly growing interdisciplinary field.

"Hopefully quantum batteries will soon no longer be a theoretical idea but something than can be built in the lab."

Quantum batteries operate using core principles of quantum mechanics, including superposition and entanglement, rather than the chemical processes used in today's batteries.

The prototype itself is a small layered organic device. It can be charged wirelessly using a laser, showing how energy could be delivered without direct physical connections.

Laser Charging and Future Energy Possibilities

Lead author Dr. James Quach, a CSIRO Science Leader, said the work points toward a new kind of energy future.

"Our proof-of-concept device showcases rapid, scalable charging and energy storage at room temperature, laying the groundwork for next-gen energy solutions," he said.

"While there's still much work to be done in quantum battery research, we've made an important move towards realizing the possibilities.

"My ultimate ambition is a future where we can charge electric cars much faster than fuel petrol cars, or charge devices over long distances wirelessly."

Moving Toward Real-World Applications

The team is now working to extend how long quantum batteries can hold their charge. Improving energy storage time will be critical for making the technology practical and commercially viable.

While still in the early stages, this breakthrough suggests that quantum batteries could one day deliver faster charging, wireless energy transfer, and performance that surpasses today's battery technologies.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough in energy storage technology with the development of a working quantum battery prototype. This innovation promises faster charging and improved energy storage capacity, which could have broad and meaningful impacts on energy systems and everyday technology use. The story is focused, detailed, and highlights a major step toward practical applications, fulfilling the criteria for inspiring good news.
articles.personality_title: Scientists create a quantum battery that charges faster as it grows
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have been looking for new ways to store energy more efficiently. Traditional batteries rely on chemical reactions to charge and release energy, but researchers are exploring quantum physics to develop better options.

**What happened** – A team from Australia’s CSIRO, RMIT University, and the University of Melbourne built a working prototype of a quantum battery. This small device uses laser light to charge, stores energy, and can release it again. Unlike normal batteries, it works using quantum physics principles like superposition and entanglement.

**Impact** – This quantum battery charges faster as it gets bigger, which is the opposite of how regular batteries behave. This means larger quantum batteries could be much more powerful and efficient. The prototype also shows wireless charging is possible, which could change how we power devices in the future.

**What's next step** – Researchers now want to make these batteries hold their charge longer and improve their performance. The goal is to develop practical quantum batteries that can be used for things like charging electric cars quickly or powering devices without wires.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists have built a small quantum battery that charges faster as it grows, showing a new way to store and deliver energy more efficiently than traditional batteries.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques créent une batterie quantique qui se recharge plus vite en grandissant
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques cherchent de nouvelles façons de stocker l'énergie plus efficacement. Les batteries traditionnelles fonctionnent grâce à des réactions chimiques, mais les chercheurs explorent la physique quantique pour développer de meilleures solutions.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Une équipe australienne du CSIRO, de l'université RMIT et de l'université de Melbourne a construit un prototype fonctionnel de batterie quantique. Ce petit appareil utilise un laser pour se recharger, stocker de l'énergie, puis la libérer. Contrairement aux batteries classiques, il fonctionne grâce aux principes de la physique quantique comme la superposition et l'intrication.

**Impact** – Cette batterie quantique se recharge plus vite en grandissant, ce qui est l'inverse des batteries normales. Cela signifie que des batteries quantiques plus grandes pourraient être bien plus puissantes et efficaces. Le prototype montre aussi qu'une recharge sans fil est possible, ce qui pourrait changer notre façon d'alimenter les appareils.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs veulent maintenant prolonger la durée de charge et améliorer les performances. Leur objectif est de créer des batteries quantiques pratiques pour recharger rapidement des voitures électriques ou alimenter des appareils sans fil.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Des scientifiques ont construit une petite batterie quantique qui se recharge plus vite en grandissant, ouvrant la voie à un stockage d'énergie plus efficace que les batteries traditionnelles.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos crean una batería cuántica que se carga más rápido al crecer
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos buscan nuevas formas de almacenar energía de manera más eficiente. Las baterías tradicionales funcionan con reacciones químicas, pero los investigadores exploran la física cuántica para desarrollar mejores opciones.

**Qué pasó** – Un equipo australiano del CSIRO, la Universidad RMIT y la Universidad de Melbourne construyó un prototipo funcional de batería cuántica. Este pequeño dispositivo usa un láser para cargarse, almacenar energía y liberarla. A diferencia de las baterías normales, funciona con principios de la física cuántica como la superposición y el entrelazamiento.

**Impacto** – Esta batería cuántica se carga más rápido a medida que crece, lo contrario a lo que sucede con las baterías tradicionales. Esto significa que las baterías cuánticas más grandes podrían ser mucho más potentes y eficientes. El prototipo también demuestra que la carga inalámbrica es posible, lo que podría cambiar cómo alimentamos los dispositivos en el futuro.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores quieren ahora hacer que estas baterías mantengan la carga por más tiempo y mejorar su rendimiento. El objetivo es desarrollar baterías cuánticas prácticas para cargar autos eléctricos rápidamente o alimentar dispositivos sin cables.

**Resumen en una frase** – Científicos construyeron una pequeña batería cuántica que se carga más rápido al crecer, mostrando una nueva forma de almacenar y entregar energía más eficiente que las baterías tradicionales.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a small, glowing layered organic device at the center, surrounded by delicate, intertwined beams of light and abstract quantum waves symbolizing superposition and entanglement, with a soft laser beam gently charging the device wirelessly in a calm, minimalistic laboratory-like setting.
